summary: I reviewed the setup secret handling, live display/player polling, host game-state refresh paths, and winner-stage validation changes for failure-path regressions. The main workflow risk is state regression: polling or unrelated game-state updates can overwrite newer number-call state, which can make the host/display/player briefly or persistently show fewer called numbers during a live cash game.
appears_sound: `src/app/api/setup/route.ts:15` hashes both the provided and expected setup secrets before `timingSafeEqual`, avoiding length-mismatch exceptions while preserving constant-time comparison.

`src/app/display/[sessionId]/display-ui.tsx:138` and `src/app/player/[sessionId]/player-ui.tsx:165` use effect cleanup with `cancelled`, `clearInterval`, and `removeEventListener`, so interval and visibility listeners are not left running after unmount.

`src/app/host/actions.ts:948` re-reads live game state before `announceWin`, rejects non-`in_progress` games, and checks the requested stage against the configured live stage before mutating win-display state.

findings.description: Unrelated or stale `game_states` updates can now regress the host's called-number list. Failure sequence: host calls a number and sees optimistic/newer state, then a heartbeat or delayed poll returns an older row image, `setCurrentGameState(payload.new)` overwrites the newer `called_numbers`, and the host may call or validate against an incorrect visible state.
findings.evidence: The diff removes the previous non-regression guard at `src/app/host/[sessionId]/[gameId]/game-control.tsx:391` and replaces it with unconditional `setCurrentGameState(payload.new)`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: If every `game_states` update is guaranteed by the database to include the latest `called_numbers` and updates are strictly ordered through one channel, the regression may not occur.
findings.what_would_confirm: Simulate a number call followed by a heartbeat/update carrying an older `numbers_called_count`, or inspect the heartbeat/action SQL to prove it cannot emit a stale row image.

findings.description: The host polling fallback can overwrite newer realtime state with an older fetch result. Failure sequence: poll A reads game state before a number call but returns slowly, realtime or poll B applies the new number, then poll A completes and unconditional `setCurrentGameState(freshState)` rolls the host UI back.
findings.evidence: The diff removes the `freshState.numbers_called_count >= prev.numbers_called_count` guard and applies `freshState` unconditionally at `src/app/host/[sessionId]/[gameId]/game-control.tsx:433`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: If Supabase requests always complete in issue order and every poll reads after all relevant writes, this would be harmless, but browser/network timing does not guarantee that.
findings.what_would_confirm: Throttle the network, trigger overlapping polls around `callNextNumber`, and verify whether a late older response can reduce `numbers_called_count` in React state.

findings.description: Player polling introduces the same late-response regression path for public game state. Failure sequence: a player receives a realtime update for a newly called number, an earlier 3-second poll then resolves with an older `game_states_public` row, and the card/history display loses the latest call until another update arrives.
findings.evidence: The new poll in `src/app/player/[sessionId]/player-ui.tsx:194` unconditionally calls `setCurrentGameState(freshState)` after each fetch, with no monotonic guard on `numbers_called_count`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The public view may only expose committed latest rows at response time, and a later poll would usually self-heal the UI after a few seconds.
findings.what_would_confirm: Run a throttled browser test with realtime enabled and overlapping polls, then assert `numbers_called_count` never decreases in the player UI.

findings.description: Display polling can roll the big-screen number display and prize text back to an older game-state snapshot. Failure sequence: the venue display applies a current call via realtime, a delayed poll returns stale `current_stage_index` or called-number data, and the public screen shows the wrong current number or stage during play.
findings.evidence: The new display poll in `src/app/display/[sessionId]/display-ui.tsx:170` unconditionally sets `currentGameState` and derives `currentPrizeText` from the fetched state.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: If the delayed-number rendering layer ignores regressive state, the user-visible current number may be protected, but that logic is outside the visible pack section.
findings.what_would_confirm: Verify the delayed display logic after line 200 and test whether `numbers_called_count` or `current_stage_index` can visually move backward under delayed polling.

findings.description: `recordWinner` now ignores the submitted `callCountAtWin` and always records the later live call count, which can misrecord a valid claim if the host calls another number before entering the winner. Failure sequence: player calls bingo at call 45, host accidentally advances to 46 before recording, `recordWinner` discards the claim-time value and persists 46, which can affect snowball eligibility and audit history.
findings.evidence: The diff adds `void callCountAtWin;` and resolves `call_count_at_win` from `liveStateRow.numbers_called_count` at `src/app/host/actions.ts:1123`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The UI may pause number calling during validation or pass `callCountAtWin` only as an untrusted stale client value, making the live count the intended source of truth.
findings.what_would_confirm: Inspect the claim/validation workflow to see whether calling is blocked from the moment a claim is raised until `recordWinner` completes.

empty_categories: No polling cleanup leak found in the changed display/player effects; intervals and visibility listeners are removed on cleanup.
No setup-secret failure-path issue found; missing secret, invalid JSON, missing email, missing service key, user lookup failure, and profile update failure all return explicit errors.
No obvious live-stage bypass found in the changed `announceWin` and `recordWinner` paths; both re-read live state and reject stage mismatches before writing.
